figures.caption: Figure 4: Study A: SIREN versus a nonparametric item-bootstrap baseline at K=10, R=5. (a) Both methods cover at the nominal level (error bars: ±1.96·SE). (b) CI width ratios are within ±0.8% of unity. The item-bootstrap is ∼ 38× slower because it reruns the full repeated-split pipeline on every resample, while SIREN runs it once and bootstraps a length-M vector.

Figure 5: Study B: Hard-selection coverage fails in the near-tie regime. (a) Empirical coverage vs. quality gap ∆; error bands are ±SE over Nsim=2,000 trials, grey band is the Monte Carlo 95% tolerance region around the nominal level. Hard (orange) drops to 89.8% at ∆=0.20; soft (blue) is stable across the sweep. (b) Winner instability π̂win and the adaptive threshold 0.10. The shaded orange band marks the empirical hard-undercoverage zone ∆ ∈ [0.10, 0.30] and aligns with the regime where π̂win crosses the threshold from above.

Figure 6: Study B: Why hard-selection bootstrap undercovers. The orange shaded area is the variance that the hard influence function ψ̂hard does not capture: the discontinuous jump of the argmax winner across splits. Soft selection has a non-zero Jacobian and therefore estimates its own variance correctly (blue lines track each other).

Table 11: Unified per-model comparison across budgets on MMLU-Pro Law with the DSPy tuner. The untuned-pipeline-default reference θ⋆B is fixed across budgets and shown once at the top. For each budget, θ⋆A is the Monte-Carlo tuned target, and all method rows report estimates θ̂A of this tuned target only. The ✓/✗marker indicates whether sign(θ̂A − θ⋆B) agrees with the true direction sign(θ⋆A − θ⋆B). M1 and M2 are merged because they are numerically identical in point estimate and directional call.

Figure 8: Study C: same-data optimism validation. Left: Optimism bias vs. library size HA at fixed HB=3 (paired item draws). Same-data reporting (red) produces bias growing roughly as σ √ 2 logH/ √ M (dashed); the repeated-split estimator (blue) has near-zero bias. Right: When two systems with identical true quality search over unequal libraries (independent item draws), same-data reporting declares the system with the larger library the winner 71–88% of the time across six pairings, despite ground-truth equality.

Figure 2: Study A: Coverage and width across M at R=5. (a) Empirical coverage of the multiplierbootstrap 95% CI. Error bars are ±SE over Nsim=2,000 trials; the grey band is the Monte Carlo 95% tolerance region around the nominal level. (b) Log–log plot of average CI width; dotted line shows the reference M−1/2 slope.
Table 3: Unified per-model comparison across budgets on MMLU-Pro Math with the DSPy tuner.

Table 13: Unified PromptEval [24] vs SIREN comparison across budgets on MMLU-Pro Law with the random search tuner. The untuned-pipeline-default reference θ⋆B is fixed across budgets and shown once at the top. For each budget, θ⋆A is the Monte-Carlo tuned target, and all method rows report estimates θ̂A of this tuned target only. The ✓/✗marker indicates whether sign(θ̂A − θ⋆B) agrees with the true direction sign(θ⋆A − θ⋆B).

Figure 7: Study B: Instability-triggered adaptive rule. (a) Coverage of hard, soft, and adaptive across ∆. Adaptive (green) improves on hard near ties but does not fully match soft at boundary points where π̂win ≈ 0.10. (b) Average CI width: adaptive coincides with hard in the stable regime (large ∆) and pulls toward soft near ties, giving a dynamic trade-off between width and coverage.

Figure 3: Study A: Effect of the number of repeated splitsR at fixedM=500,K=10. (a) Coverage is stable across R (all within the MC 95% band). (b) Width contracts quickly up to R=5, with diminishing returns beyond.

Figure 1: Overview of the SIREN pipeline. (a)–(b) tuning and shortlist freezing; (c) score tensor on the split benchmarks; (d)–(e) repeated-split inference and aggregation into the procedure-level estimate θ̃; (f)–(g) multiplier-bootstrap uncertainty quantification and final CI output.
